entities.name: saṃjñā-śabda
entities.cite: the name-word, at the moment of definition, has its meaning

entities.name: prathamā
entities.cite: takes the case of independent standing

entities.name: genitive case
entities.cite: The genitive construal, asya iti, 'of this', is not thereby lost

entities.name: vyatireka
entities.cite: it arises tadarthat eva, out of that same meaning

axioms.name: has_meaning
axioms.cite: the name-word, at the moment of definition, has its meaning

axioms.name: justified_by_meaning
axioms.cite: The nominative in the defining rule is justified arthavattvat — the name-word, at the moment of definition, has its meaning

axioms.name: arises_from_meaning
axioms.cite: it arises tadarthat eva, out of that same meaning

axioms.name: expresses_moments
axioms.cite: The two cases thus express two logical moments of one act — the name in itself, the name as belonging

denials.name: separate_derivations
denials.cite: once the assignment has related name to named and the name can be viewed as the named item's property

claims.name: justified_by_meaning
claims.cite: the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word

claims.name: arises_from_meaning
claims.cite: the differentiation 'of this' arises precisely from that meaning

claims.name: expresses_moments
claims.cite: There, because it possesses the meaning, the first case-ending is prescribed after the name-word; and the differentiation 'of this' arises precisely from that meaning

rejected_readings.label: unrelated_cases_error
rejected_readings.rendering: The nominative and genitive case-endings are independently justified grammatical forms with no intrinsic connection to each other.
rejected_readings.why: Treats the two cases as grammatically arbitrary rather than expressing two logical moments of a single naming act. The commentary explicitly states they express unified structure through the naming relation.

rejected_readings.label: genitive_denied
rejected_readings.rendering: Only the nominative case-ending applies to the name-word; the genitive 'of this' is not a valid grammatical form.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly states the differentiation arises from the meaning. Denying the genitive violates the unified structure of naming that both cases express.

notes: The verse resolves a grammatical puzzle (nominative vs genitive) into an ontological claim about the two-fold logic of naming. The contract captures the name-word's dual case-marking as reflecting the duality of the naming relation itself: name-as-independent (nominative) and name-as-property (genitive), both grounded in meaning.
